title: Traveling With Centrino
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Hands-on review after two months of travel with a Gateway 450 Intel Centrino notebook. Covers the Pentium M + chipset + WiFi Centrino platform architecture, 15in 1400x1050 screen trade-offs, 6.2lb weight versus preferred thin-and-light 4lb class, 1.5 GHz performance versus Pentium III/P4 desktops, battery life including DVD playback plus work day, WiFi range improvements including a Cometa McDonald's hotspot picked up from a 15th-floor hotel room across the street, and corporate 3-notebooks-per-7-desktops adoption forecast.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous first-person field test of Intel's $300M Centrino launch platform at the moment it entered corporate notebook rollouts; pairs vendor narrative with real travel use.
relevance_rationale: Thin-and-light versus desktop-replacement trade-off, battery/WiFi use patterns, and mobile productivity framing remain directly applicable; specific hardware dated.
prescience_rationale: Correctly forecast Centrino becoming mainstream for business laptops and WiFi infrastructure rapid growth in hotels and meeting spaces; called thin-and-light/tablet/executive-performance variants that Intel shipped.
